verdicts.reasoning: Emotional axis (60%): Strong Zelda palette — saturated Link-green body with gold/yellow outer ring reads warm, bright, and friendly. Circular silhouette is round and toy-like, not threatening. Color choices (green + gold) are thematically on-point for a Hyrule UI confirm icon. Score ~8/10 emotional. Technical axis (40%): Pixel-grid-snapped with visible 1px hard outlines at zoom; 2–3 tone hard shading is present (dark green shadow, mid green body, lighter highlight). No anti-aliasing detected. However, the outer ring dithering/scatter produces a slightly noisy read at native 1x size, and the internal motif (sword/check shape) is ambiguous at small scale — detail density is at the edge of legibility. Denzi reference items show crisper internal silhouettes and stronger outline-to-fill contrast. Score ~6/10 technical. Weighted: 0.6*8 + 0.4*6 = 7.2 → 7. Marginal: passes emotional criteria, minor technical refinement needed on outline clarity and interior readability.

At 1x the icon reads as a tiny green smudge — the checkmark-in-circle motif is not instantly legible and the silhouette has no distinct toy-charm shape. At 4x the pixel construction is technically clean: green circle base with a yellow pixel border ring and a dark checkmark stroke, which gives decent contrast on both the mid-tone beige bg and the dark bg. On the light bg it holds reasonably well. However the form is angular-pixelated rather than round/soft — the circle feels jagged and the overall impression is 'generic retro icon' rather than friendly toy charm. No visible personality or sticker-test appeal. Passes basic contrast but fails the charm and 1x-readability bars.

Icon uses saturated forest green arrows with bright amber/gold pixel accent dots and a light mint highlight — all warm, saturated hues that align well with the Echoes-tuned palette greens and golds. No cold blues, no neon, no near-black or pure grey in the icon itself. The green-gold pairing is harmonious and classically warm. However the palette is narrow (2-3 hues) and no shadow pixels are visible to verify colored-dark shadow discipline. Background display panels in the zoom sheet are neutral dark/charcoal but these are compositional context, not icon colors. Mood reads BRIGHT/WARM with good saturation; falls just short of a confident pass due to limited chromatic richness and unverifiable shadow quality.

The icon is placed on a noticeably bright beige/cream square tile that creates extreme contrast against the surrounding dark teal game grid. While this makes the position locatable in under 0.5s, the readability credit belongs to the jarring background tile rather than the icon itself. The icon (small green/yellow pixel art gem) is visually noisy and undersized within the tile. The beige background does not match any other UI element in the scene and clashes severely with the Echoes dark-teal color palette — it reads as a placeholder tile or foreign asset, not a native UI element. No outline blending issue, but the integration failure is fundamental: the tile background is the dominant visual, not the icon glyph. Tonal clash and non-native feel prevent a passing score.

The confirm icon sprite sits in a cream/parchment-toned square frame and uses warm green and gold/yellow pixel art colors — both align with the target palette of warm greens/yellows/parchment. No grimdark, horror, or cold-corporate elements are present. However the pixel-art execution reads as generic retro-RPG rather than distinctly cute fairy-tale; there are no sparkle, floral, or whimsical shape cues that would anchor it to the 'bright sunny magical toy diorama' mood. The sprite is inoffensive and does not actively break tone, but it also does not positively reinforce it. Marginal pass — acceptable if upgraded with a rounder shape or a small sparkle detail.

The system_confirm_icon_v00 (green gem sprite) is composited on a large light-colored square panel (~80x80px visible) in the center of the scene. The icon sprite itself is approximately 28-32px, near or at the ≤24x24 HUD icon spec limit. The oversized white card background dominates relative to the 32x32 player-scale entities visible on the left, disrupting the toy diorama aesthetic. The icon sprite proportion is acceptable but the panel framing inflates perceived visual weight. Perspective is consistent with top-down view. No overlap with critical gameplay elements, but the card panel feels like a wallpaper UI overlay rather than an integrated diorama element.

8x zoom reveals a pixel-art octagonal badge with green/yellow/white interior forming a checkmark-like pattern. Positive: limited palette, no gradient shading, no noise in solid fill areas, semantic intent (confirm checkmark) broadly legible. Issues found: (1) Outline inconsistency — border pixels alternate between 1px and 2px thickness on diagonal segments of the octagon, especially upper-left quadrant vs lower-right; (2) Edge AA blending — several boundary pixels between the icon shape and the beige canvas background carry intermediate tone values rather than hard pixel steps, indicating sub-pixel compositing not typical of hand-drawn pixel art; (3) Drop shadow irregularity — the dark shadow cluster at bottom-right is unevenly distributed, with isolated shadow pixels that don't follow a consistent offset vector; (4) Mild asymmetry — left and right halves of the badge differ by 1-2 pixel offsets on the outer boundary. None of these individually are fatal, but the combination of AA blending on solid shapes and the irregular shadow strongly suggest AI generation or automated upscale post-processing rather than clean hand-authored pixel art. No color bleed, no melting details, no pattern tiling artifacts detected.

Candidate is a mosaic-textured sphere (globe/checkmark icon) while peers are Denzi-style clean pixel-art helmets. The core rendering paradigm is different: peers use hard 1-2px black outlines with flat shading and clear pixel structure; candidate has no visible crisp outline and instead uses a dithered/mosaic tile pattern across its surface. Palette does not overlap — peers are grey/silver with occasional warm accents (red plume, gold trim, teal), candidate is green/yellow/white mosaic. Detail density is higher on the candidate due to the fragmented tile texture. Shadow direction is unreadable on the candidate due to the mosaic pattern fragmenting any coherent shading gradient. Highlight is scattered across multiple mosaic tiles rather than a single clean specular hotspot. Charm level is a mild mismatch: peers read as utilitarian game equipment icons, candidate reads as a system/UI globe — different 'feel' family. Even accounting for item-type difference, the fundamental rendering style (mosaic texture vs flat pixel art) disqualifies this candidate from the peer set.

Candidate (right, 4x): a small circular pixel-art icon, warm green + gold/yellow on parchment background. Positive signals: palette is warm, parchment context is cozy/storybook, no grimdark or edgy elements, no desaturation, no realistic shading, nothing fan-service or occult. Concern: the icon reads as a generic gem/seal — it lacks the expressive, handcrafted, toy-diorama whimsy expected of a Nintendo-family-friendly confirm icon (think bright ribbon-seal or chibi stamp energy rather than a flat orb). Peer references (left) show metallic knight/viking helmets that lean toward dark-armory aesthetic — this contextual pull toward dungeon-crawler is a background risk for the asset set, though the candidate itself avoids it. Net: warm and safe but insufficiently charming; qualifies as marginal rather than a clean pass.
